LAW AND MOTION 302, PLAINTIFF GEOFFREY MURRY'S MOTION FOR SANCTIONS PURSUANT TO CCP 128.7 IS GRANTED. THE COURT STRIKES THE THIRD AMENDED CROSS-COMPLAINT WITH LEAVE TO AMEND. HENDRICKS IS INSTRUCTED TO REMOVE ALL ALLEGATIONS THAT ALLEGE THAT MURRY WAS IN FACT A "PARTNER OR SHAREHOLDER." GIVEN THE COURT'S POLICY TO HEAR CASES ON THE MERITS, HENDRICKS HAS 20 DAYS LEAVE TO ALLEGE CLAIMS THAT ARE CONSISTENT WITH THE FACTUAL RECORD. BOTH PARTIES' REQUESTS FOR MONETARY SANCTIONS ARE DENIED. ORDER IS SIGNED. (SEE SCANNED ORDER FOR DETAILS). JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN, CLERK: M. MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION / Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment And Proof Of Service
Off Calendar Oct-31-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Cari Cohorn, Esq. 415-993-9005 of Cohorn Law for Plaintiff.
Clariza Garcia, Esq. 510-8447766 of Garcia Dewalt LLP for Defendant John Hendricks who is also present.

RULING - A R G U E D. Defendant argues, requests ruling on objection evidence. Plaintiff responds. The Court shares its views. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT JOHN HENDRICKS, HENDRICKS LAW P.C.'s Motion For Summary Judgment OR ALTERNATIVELY MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION OF ISSUES.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Defendants Hendricks Law P.C. and John T. Hendricks' motion for summary judgment is denied.

The motion for summary adjudication is denied as to the Labor Code claims, UCL claim, and breach of the Of Counsel contract claim (causes of action 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 8, 9, and 10). There is a triable issue of material fact regarding whether the parties had an employment relationship until Mr. Murry's termination on April 10, 2017.

On January 19, 2011, Mr. Murry began working for the Law Offices of John T. Hendricks (a sole proprietorship) under the Of Counsel agreement. The agreement provides that Mr. Murry is an independent contractor, but Mr. Murry presents evidence showing that Mr. Hendricks controlled his work. (Murry Decl., 3-5, 7, 12.) Control is the touchstone of an employment relationship and there is a disputed issue of fact whether Mr. Murry worked as an employee under the Of Counsel agreement. (See Dynamex Operations W. v. Superior Court (2018) 4 Cal.5th 903, 929 [explaining how the degree of control has historically been the most important factor in determining an employment relationship].) Mr. Hendricks mischaracterizes Mr. Murry's deposition testimony at 64:23-65:12. Mr. Murry testified that he exercised discretion with regard to how matters were handled, not that Hendricks did not supervise his work.

Thereafter, the parties discussed making Mr. Murry a shareholder in the professional corporation, but the shareholder agreement was never consummated. (Murry Decl., 6.) Murry subsequently continued to work in the same fashion, i.e., as an alleged employee, until his termination. (Murry Decl., 7 and 12.) Defendants' continued (and inconsistent) characterizations of Mr. Murry as a "shareholder," "partner," and "owner" of the law firm are inaccurate. The evidence discloses that Mr. Murry had an opportunity to purchase shares in the professional corporation but the purchase never occurred. (UMF 32.) Further, there is no competent evidence that the parties ever entered into a partnership agreement or formed a "partnership." (UMF 31.) Partnerships and professional corporations are different animals. The Moscone-Knox Professional Corporation Act governs professional corporations. (See Corps. Code 13400-13410.) The Uniform Partnership Act governs partnerships. (See Corps. Code 16100 et seq.)

The court denies summary adjudication on the right of publicity claims (causes of action 6 and 7). Defendants cite 74 facts in their statement of undisputed material facts to show that the claims "cannot be established" within the meaning of Code of Civil Procedure, 437c(p)(2). Of those facts, only facts 71 and 72 appear germane to these claims. Those facts show that defendants purportedly deactivated their prior website that referenced Mr. Murry. (Hendricks Decl., 17.) Mr. Murry's evidence shows that information regarding him continued to be displayed after he ceased working with defendants, however, and there is a triable issue of material fact regarding damage, if any. (Cohorn Decl., 5-6; Exs. H, I.)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

MOTION - Notice Of Motion And Motion For Sanctions Pursuant To Ccp 128.7
Off Calendar Sep-23-2019 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF GEOFFREY MURRY's MOTION For Sanctions Pursuant To Ccp 128.7.

Plaintiff Geoffrey Murry's motion for sanctions is granted.

Pursuant to Code of Civ. Proc., 128.7(b)(3), Hendricks' factual contentions must have evidentiary support or are likely to have such support. The record shows that although the parties contemplated that Murry would become a shareholder in the future, going so far as to change the name of the firm and enter into an interim stock purchase agreement, at all times the law firm was a professional corporation and Murry never actually became a de jure shareholder. (Cohorn Decl., Exs. A - E.) The corporate minutes, signed by Hendricks, establish that Hendricks consistently owned 100% of the outstanding shares. (Id. at Ex. E ["John T. Hendricks, the Corporation's sole shareholder".) Hendricks nevertheless alleges in the Third Amended Cross-Complaint that Murry was, in fact, a "partner and shareholder" in the firm. (E.g., TAC 10, 11, 15, 16, 35, 37, 45, 56, 57, 58, 60.) Moreover, other allegations in the Third Amended Cross-Complaint show that Hendricks knew that Murry never became a shareholder. Hendricks admits that Murry never paid the $1,000 to purchase the shares. (TAC, 10, 13["Murry never funded his check for his initial stock purchase of $1,000 and he never made an additional stock purchase."].) A number of claims against Murry in the Third Amended Cross-Complaint are based on that false allegation.

The court strikes the Third Amended Cross-Complaint with leave to amend. Hendricks is instructed to remove all allegations that allege that Murry was in fact a "partner or shareholder." Given the court's policy to hear cases on the merits, Hendricks has 20 days leave to allege claims that are consistent with the factual record. Both parties' requests for monetary sanctions are denied.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-28-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

CROSS DEFENDANT GEOFFREY MURRY'S DEMURRER TO DEFENDANT JOHN T. HENDRICK'S AND HENDRICKS LAW, P.C.'S 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT.

1st (Breach of Contract: Of Counsel Agreement). SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND. The FACC pleads that the parties operated under this agreement "until January 2014." (Id. at 3:26.) The first cross-complaint was not filed until July 2018, seemingly outside the four-year statute of limitations. (See CCP 337.)
2nd (Breach of Contract: Stock Purchase/Confidentiality Agreements). OVERRULED. This cause of action is adequately pled. Murry argues for a level of pleading particularity not required by California law. (See, e.g., Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311, 318.)
3rd (Breach of Oral Contract): OVERRULED. Murry again argues for a level of pleading particularity that is not required, and causes of action may be pled in the alternative. (Burks v. Poppy Const. Co. (1962) 57 Cal.2d 463, 470.)
4th (Breach of Implied-In-Fact Contract): SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND. The FACC does not adequately plead this cause in the alternative. (See Klein v. Chevron USA (2012) 202 Cal.App.4th 1342, 1388-89.)
5th (Breach of Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing). OVERRULED. Again, Murry argues for a level of pleading particularity that is not required.
6th (Breach of Fiduciary Duty). OVERRULED. The FACC's allegations regarding the parties' relationships are adequate for the pleading stage. (See, e.g., FACC 9-11, 50.)
9th & 10th (Fraud, Negligent Misrepresentation). OVERRULED. These causes are pled with adequate particularity. (See, e.g., FACC 76, 82.)
11th (Business & Professions Code 17200). OVERRULED. This cause is adequately pled.
